_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology from Lustmann 1990 (PMID 2387991) and Escudier UK prevalence 2008 (PMID 19010469). Distribution (80% submandibular, 20% parotid) per Marchal Otolaryngol Clin North Am 2009. Sialendoscopy outcomes from Iro Eur Arch Otorhinolaryngol 2009 (PMID 19015846) and Capaccio Otol HNS 2007. ESWL evidence from Iro Laryngoscope 1992 and Capaccio Laryngoscope 2004 (PMID 15064620). Imaging guidance from Brown BMJ 2016 (PMID 27542384). Management algorithm follows Capaccio Acta Otorhinolaryngol Ital 2018 and AAO-HNS practice patterns.

h1Title: Salivary Gland Stones: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis & Treatment
heroOverview: Salivary gland stones (sialolithiasis) are calcified concretions that form within a major salivary gland duct or parenchyma, obstructing saliva flow and producing the characteristic story of painful gland swelling that flares at mealtimes and settles between meals. Population-based prevalence is approximately 1 case per 10,000-30,000 per year (Escudier 2008; Lustmann 1990), with roughly 12 in 1,000 adults harboring asymptomatic small stones found incidentally on imaging. About 80-90% form in the submandibular gland and Wharton's duct because of viscous mucinous saliva, alkaline pH, and a long, upward-running duct; 5-20% form in the parotid in Stensen's duct, and under 2% in sublingual or minor glands. Without treatment, stones cause recurrent infection, gland fibrosis, and eventual atrophy. Modern care is gland-preserving: roughly 80% of stones are now removed by sialendoscopy or combined endoscopic-transoral approaches, with extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy and traditional gland excision reserved for select cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Annual symptomatic incidence 1 per 10,000-30,000 in Western populations (Escudier 2008); asymptomatic small stones in roughly 1.2% of adults on imaging series
keyStats.demographics: Male:female ratio approximately 2:1; submandibular stones account for 80-90%, parotid 5-20%, sublingual under 1%
keyStats.avgAge: Peak age 30-60 years; pediatric cases occur but are rare (under 5% of sialolithiasis)
keyStats.globalCases: No reliable global figure; extrapolation suggests several million prevalent cases worldwide, with substantial under-diagnosis

definition: Salivary gland stones (ICD-10: K11.5, sialolithiasis) are calcified deposits composed predominantly of calcium phosphate and calcium carbonate within an organic matrix of glycoproteins, mucopolysaccharides, and cellular debris. The condition results from a combination of saliva stasis, increased viscosity, alkaline pH, and a nidus (sloughed epithelium, bacteria, or food debris) on which mineral deposition occurs. The submandibular gland is preferentially affected because Wharton's duct is long (5-6 cm), curves upward against gravity, has a narrow papilla orifice, and produces mucinous saliva with relatively high calcium and bicarbonate. Parotid stones form within Stensen's duct or the gland parenchyma and tend to be smaller and multiple. Sublingual and minor-gland stones are uncommon. The condition is one of the obstructive sialadenopathies and is distinct from autoimmune sialadenitis (Sjögren syndrome), viral parotitis (mumps), and salivary gland tumors. ENT, oral and maxillofacial, and oral medicine specialists manage diagnosis and treatment, increasingly through minimally invasive endoscopic techniques. Recurrent obstructive sialadenitis from sialolithiasis is the most common cause of recurrent swelling of a major salivary gland in adults.

typesClassification.description: 80-90% of cases. Stones tend to be radiopaque, often large (over 5 mm), and located in the duct (distal, hilar, or intraglandular). Classic mealtime swelling under the jaw is the typical presentation.
5-20% of cases. Stones are smaller (often under 5 mm), more often multiple, frequently radiolucent, and may be missed on plain radiographs. Pre-auricular swelling at mealtimes is typical.
Under 1% of cases; presents with floor-of-mouth swelling. Often associated with ranula or minor-gland stones.
Stone causes rapid-onset gland swelling, severe pain at meals, and may progress to bacterial superinfection with fever, purulent ductal discharge, and overlying erythema.
Recurrent partial obstruction over months to years produces low-grade swelling, post-prandial discomfort, and progressive gland fibrosis with reduced saliva output and increased infection risk.

primarySymptoms: Painful swelling of the affected gland that develops within minutes of starting a meal, peaks during eating, and gradually resolves over 1-2 hours (mealtime syndrome) — the most reliable clinical clue.
Palpable hard mass under the jaw (submandibular) or in front of and below the ear (parotid) that may be tender on bimanual examination of the floor of mouth.
Recurrent episodes of acute gland swelling with sour, gritty, or salty taste at mealtimes and intermittent pus from the ductal papilla.
Visible stone protruding at the ductal orifice (Wharton's or Stensen's), especially in floor-of-mouth bimanual examination.
Reduced salivary flow and a sense of dry mouth on the affected side as obstruction progresses.
Acute bacterial sialadenitis with fever, erythema, induration, purulent discharge from the duct, and trismus, particularly when obstruction is prolonged or incomplete.
Persistent firm gland on the affected side from chronic fibrosis, sometimes raising concern for tumor.
Foul taste in the mouth from intermittent purulent ductal discharge.
Difficulty opening the mouth fully and tenderness on chewing in advanced cases.
Asymptomatic detection on dental or ENT imaging in 1-2% of routine scans, often in older patients.
earlyWarningSigns: A brief tightness or fullness under the jaw or in front of the ear that comes on with the first bite of a meal and settles afterward
Intermittent sour or gritty taste when starting to eat sour or acidic foods (lemon, vinegar)
Mild tenderness in the floor of mouth or pre-auricular region without obvious mass
Occasional small white particle expelled into the mouth, particularly during chewing
Recurrent mild swelling that resolves with massage of the gland or hot drinks
emergencySigns: Severe gland swelling with fever above 38.5 °C, marked erythema, and induration — suspect bacterial sialadenitis or abscess requiring urgent antibiotics and possible drainage
Floor-of-mouth swelling with elevation of the tongue and difficulty swallowing — exclude Ludwig's angina, a deep-neck-space emergency
Marked trismus, drooling, and respiratory distress — possible deep-neck-space extension
Unilateral firm parotid mass with facial nerve weakness — consider malignant tumor, not stone, and arrange urgent imaging and biopsy
Persistent severe pain with poor response to oral antibiotics over 48-72 hours — admit for IV antibiotics and surgical drainage

causes.cause: Salivary stasis in a slow-flowing, gravity-defying duct (submandibular)
causes.description: Wharton's duct runs upward against gravity for 5-6 cm, has a narrow papilla, and drains a gland that produces viscous mucinous saliva. Slow flow allows mineral and organic material to accumulate on micro-niduses.

causes.cause: High mucinous content and alkaline pH of submandibular saliva
causes.description: Submandibular saliva is more mucinous, richer in calcium, and slightly more alkaline than parotid saliva, favoring precipitation of calcium phosphate (hydroxyapatite, brushite) and calcium carbonate. This explains the disproportionate submandibular site predilection.

Insufficient fluid intake, hot climates, diuretic use, and chronic systemic disease decrease saliva volume and increase concentration of stone-forming minerals. Patients with sialolithiasis often report low water intake.
Tricyclic antidepressants, antihistamines, antipsychotics, alpha-blockers, and diuretics reduce salivary flow and predispose to stone formation, particularly in older adults on polypharmacy.
Recurrent infection, autoimmune (Sjögren syndrome), and post-radiation gland dysfunction create slow flow, ductal stenosis, and epithelial debris that act as stone niduses.

causes.cause: Gout and disorders of calcium and uric acid metabolism
causes.description: Uncommon but recognized; some patients with recurrent stones have hypercalcemia, hyperuricosuria, or primary hyperparathyroidism. Screening is recommended in recurrent or multiple stones.

riskFactors.description: Male:female ratio approximately 2:1 across most series, possibly reflecting differences in salivary composition and hydration patterns.
Peak incidence in middle adulthood; pediatric and elderly presentations are less common but recognized.
Chronic mild dehydration concentrates saliva and increases stone formation risk. Athletes, outdoor workers, and patients on diuretics are particularly affected.
Cigarette smoking is associated with reduced salivary flow and increased ductal calcification in observational studies; quitting reduces recurrence risk after stone removal.
Anticholinergics, tricyclic antidepressants, antihistamines, antipsychotics, and diuretics reduce salivary flow and concentrate precipitates. Medication review is a low-cost preventive intervention.
Ductal stenosis from previous infection or instrumentation creates stasis that predisposes to new stone formation.
Disorders of mineral and uric acid metabolism increase risk of recurrent or multiple stones; screening with calcium, parathyroid hormone, and uric acid is recommended in recurrent disease.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 30-60 are most often affected; pediatric cases (under 5% of total) typically involve submandibular stones in school-age children.
Men outnumber women approximately 2:1 in most published series across Europe, North America, and Asia.
Submandibular gland affected in 80-90%; parotid in 5-20%; sublingual under 1%.
Incidence is broadly similar across geographies; some hot-climate populations report higher rates, likely related to dehydration.
Recurrence after gland-preserving treatment is reported in 5-15% over 5 years; risk is reduced by maintaining hydration and addressing systemic risk factors.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is usually possible from history alone. The triad of recurrent mealtime swelling, post-prandial pain, and a palpable mass in the affected gland is highly specific for sialolithiasis. Bimanual examination of the floor of mouth — one finger inside, the other outside — palpates large submandibular stones and assesses ductal tenderness. Inspection of the ductal papilla may show a visible stone, purulent discharge, or stenosis. Once history and exam suggest stones, imaging confirms location, size, and number. Plain dental radiographs (occlusal view) detect approximately 80% of submandibular stones but only 60% of parotid stones because parotid stones are more often radiolucent. Ultrasound is the first-line imaging modality in most centers because it is non-invasive, available, and approximately 85-94% sensitive and 90-97% specific for stones over 2 mm (Brown 2016). Sialography — injection of iodinated contrast into the duct — was the traditional standard but is now replaced by MR sialography or CT sialography for non-invasive ductal mapping. Cone-beam CT detects radiopaque stones and small intraglandular stones. Diagnostic sialendoscopy (a 0.8-1.6 mm semi-rigid endoscope) is both diagnostic and therapeutic, performed under local or general anesthesia. Salivary scintigraphy and FDG-PET are reserved for atypical cases. Important differential diagnoses to exclude are salivary gland tumor (firm, persistent, non-fluctuant), Sjögren syndrome (bilateral, dry eyes and mouth, autoantibodies), and chronic recurrent juvenile parotitis (pediatric, no stones, sialectasis on imaging).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes the diagnosis of obstructive sialadenitis and often palpates large stones in the floor of mouth
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Office visit 10-15 minutes. The clinician inspects the ductal papillae and palpates the gland with one finger inside the mouth and one outside; uncomfortable but not painful in most patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging; detects stones over 2 mm with 85-94% sensitivity and 90-97% specificity; assesses gland for fibrosis or abscess
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10-20 minutes lying down with gel applied to the neck and pre-auricular area. No needles, no contrast, no radiation. Same-day result by radiologist or trained ENT surgeon.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates radiopaque stones in submandibular and parotid ducts; inexpensive and widely available
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Quick dental X-ray, under 5 minutes. Sensitivity approximately 80% for submandibular stones, 60% for parotid stones; small or radiolucent stones may be missed.

diagnosticTests.test: Cone-beam CT or non-contrast CT of neck/face
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects nearly all radiopaque stones with precise location and size, including intraglandular and small stones
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 5-10 minutes scan. Lower radiation than helical CT for cone-beam scanners. Particularly useful before sialendoscopy or surgery planning.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct endoscopic visualization of the ductal system; identifies stones, stenoses, mucus plugs, and ductal changes; allows immediate treatment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Office or operating room procedure under local or general anesthesia. The duct is dilated and a 0.8-1.6 mm endoscope passed. Procedure takes 30-90 minutes; mild swelling for 1-3 days afterward.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive ductal mapping when sialendoscopy is unavailable or stones are suspected in non-visualized branches; better than conventional sialography for radiolucent stones
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in the scanner. MR is contrast-free for sialography; CT may require iodinated contrast into the duct.

diagnosticTests.test: Bloods for calcium, PTH, uric acid
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screen for hypercalcemia, primary hyperparathyroidism, and hyperuricemia in recurrent or multiple stones
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single morning fasting blood draw; results in 24-72 hours.

treatmentOverview: Modern care is gland-preserving and stepwise. Conservative measures — increased hydration, gland massage, sour-candy (sialogogue) stimulation, warm compresses, and analgesia — clear small stones (under 3-4 mm) in up to 50% of cases. If conservative treatment fails or stones are larger, the procedure is matched to stone size and location. Sialendoscopy alone removes stones up to about 5 mm in the distal duct using baskets, graspers, or microforceps under direct vision. For stones up to 7-8 mm, sialendoscopy is combined with intracorporeal laser fragmentation (holmium, thulium) or transoral incisional retrieval (especially for hilar submandibular stones in the floor of mouth). Combined endoscopic-transoral approaches achieve gland preservation in over 90% of cases in experienced centers (Iro 2009). Extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy (ESWL), available in select centers, fragments stones up to 7 mm, with stone-free rates of 35-50% at one session and 60-80% over multiple sessions (Capaccio 2004). Pneumatic intracorporeal lithotripsy is an alternative. Traditional submandibular gland excision (sialoadenectomy) was historically the standard and is now reserved for very large, deep intraglandular stones, end-stage gland fibrosis, or failure of minimally invasive techniques; it cures the disease but carries marginal mandibular and lingual nerve risk. Parotid gland excision is similarly reserved for the rare case of irreversible damage. Acute sialadenitis is managed with oral antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate or clindamycin), warm compresses, analgesia, and sialogogues; abscess requires drainage. Recurrence prevention focuses on hydration, sialogogues with meals, and management of contributing systemic risk factors.

medicalTreatments.name: Conservative management (hydration, sialogogues, warm compress, gland massage, NSAIDs)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for small stones and acute flares. Patients are instructed to drink 2-3 L water daily, suck sugar-free lemon drops to stimulate flow, apply warm compresses, and massage the gland from posterior to anterior. NSAIDs control pain and reduce inflammation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Spontaneous stone passage in up to 50% of stones under 3 mm within weeks (Capaccio 2018); symptomatic improvement in 70-80% of acute flares

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotic therapy for acute sialadenitis
medicalTreatments.description: Empirical oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 7-10 days for uncomplicated bacterial sialadenitis; clindamycin in penicillin-allergic patients. IV therapy for systemic illness or abscess.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in 70-80% within 48-72 hours; failure of response prompts imaging for abscess

medicalTreatments.name: Sialendoscopy with basket or grasper retrieval
medicalTreatments.description: Minimally invasive endoscopic removal under local or general anesthesia. Effective for stones up to about 5 mm in the distal duct. Avoids facial incision and preserves gland function.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stone-free rate 80-95% for amenable stones in experienced centers (Iro 2009, Marchal 2009)

medicalTreatments.name: Sialendoscopy with laser or pneumatic fragmentation
medicalTreatments.description: Holmium:YAG or thulium laser delivered through the endoscope fragments larger or impacted stones into pieces that can be removed or flushed. Pneumatic lithotripsy is an alternative.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stone-free rate 70-85% across single or staged sessions (Schrötzlmair 2015)

medicalTreatments.name: Combined endoscopic-transoral approach
medicalTreatments.description: For hilar submandibular stones, sialendoscopy locates the stone while a small intraoral incision over the duct allows direct retrieval. Particularly effective for stones 5-15 mm.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stone-free rate 90-97% with gland preservation in over 90% (Marchal 2009)

medicalTreatments.name: Extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy (ESWL)
medicalTreatments.description: Non-invasive focused shockwaves fragment stones up to 7 mm. Typically 1-3 sessions of 30-60 minutes each. Less effective for submandibular than parotid stones; available in select centers.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stone-free rate 35-50% after one session, 60-80% after multiple sessions (Capaccio Laryngoscope 2004)

surgicalOptions.name: Transoral sialolithotomy
surgicalOptions.description: Direct intraoral surgical removal of large palpable submandibular stones via incision over the floor-of-mouth duct under local or general anesthesia. Day-case procedure; risk of lingual nerve injury under 5% in experienced hands.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone-free rate 90-98% for amenable stones; recurrence under 10% with hydration and sialogogue advice

surgicalOptions.name: Submandibular gland excision (sialoadenectomy)
surgicalOptions.description: Open removal of the gland through a transcervical incision under general anesthesia. Reserved for deep intraglandular stones, end-stage fibrosis, or failure of gland-preserving techniques. Cures the disease but sacrifices the gland.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure of obstructive symptoms in over 95%; marginal mandibular nerve injury 1-8%, lingual nerve injury 1-4%, hypoglossal injury under 1% in experienced centers

surgicalOptions.name: Parotidectomy (partial or superficial)
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for large intraglandular parotid stones with irreversible gland damage. Facial nerve dissection adds complexity; minimally invasive approaches are preferred wherever feasible.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure of obstructive symptoms 90-95%; transient facial nerve weakness in 10-30%, permanent weakness in 2-5%

alternativeTreatments.name: Sialogogue drugs and sugar-free citrus candies
alternativeTreatments.description: Stimulate salivary flow, helping flush small stones and prevent recurrence. Pilocarpine or cevimeline are used in patients with concurrent hyposalivation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin injection for chronic recurrent sialadenitis
alternativeTreatments.description: Selected refractory cases of chronic sialadenitis benefit from intraglandular botulinum toxin to reduce salivary output and recurrent flares; emerging adjunct, not first line.

whySeeSpecialist: An ENT or oral and maxillofacial surgeon should evaluate any patient with recurrent gland swelling, palpable mass, or imaging-confirmed stone over 3-4 mm. Specialist input is essential to select between conservative therapy, sialendoscopy, ESWL, transoral removal, and gland excision.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose an ENT or oral and maxillofacial surgeon with formal sialendoscopy training and a personal annual volume of at least 30 cases. High-volume sialendoscopy units consistently report gland-preservation rates above 90%. For very large or intraglandular stones, ESWL availability is an additional advantage.
hospitalCriteria: Sialendoscopy unit with at least 0.8 mm and 1.3 mm semi-rigid endoscopes and basket/grasper instruments
Holmium or thulium intracorporeal laser availability for stone fragmentation
On-site high-resolution ultrasound and cone-beam CT
Oral and maxillofacial or ENT surgery cover for transoral and open procedures
Multidisciplinary sialendoscopy case conference for complex stones
keyFacilities: Salivary gland disease clinics within otolaryngology
Hospitals with ESWL programs for salivary stones
preventionStrategies: Drink 2-3 L of water daily, more in hot climates or with diuretic medication.
Use sugar-free citrus drops, gum, or vitamin C tablets at meals after a known stone episode to keep saliva flowing.
Review medications with a clinician and reduce xerogenic drugs where possible.
Stop smoking; tobacco reduces salivary flow and worsens sialolithiasis recurrence.
Maintain meticulous oral hygiene to reduce ductal infection risk.
Screen for hypercalcemia and hyperuricemia in patients with recurrent or multiple stones.
lifestyleModifications: Massage the affected gland from posterior to anterior several times per day during flare-ups.
Apply warm compresses to the gland for 10-15 minutes before meals during acute episodes.
Sip water with each meal and chew thoroughly to maximize sialogogue effect.
Avoid prolonged dehydration and high-salt fasting diets that thicken saliva.
Maintain oral and dental hygiene including twice-daily brushing and flossing.
Attend follow-up ultrasound at 3 and 12 months after stone removal to confirm clearance.

recommended: 2-3 L water daily, more in hot weather or with diuretic therapy
Citrus fruits and sour fresh produce to stimulate saliva (lemon, kiwi, pineapple)
Sugar-free chewing gum to maintain ductal flow between meals
Calcium and vitamin D from a balanced diet — there is no evidence that dietary restriction reduces stone formation
avoid: Sustained dehydration, excess alcohol, and high-caffeine beverages without compensating water intake
Hard or sharp food during the acute phase, which can aggravate ductal trauma
Anticholinergic over-the-counter medicines (some cold and motion-sickness drugs) without medical advice

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise is encouraged, with attention to hydration during prolonged exertion in heat. Avoid contact sports without a mouthguard for 2-4 weeks after intraoral surgery. Resumed normal activity within 24-48 hours after most sialendoscopy procedures.
dailyManagement: Drink water regularly through the day; aim for pale urine.
Use sialogogues (lemon drops, sugar-free gum) at the start of each main meal.
Massage the affected gland 2-3 times daily during flare-ups.
Inspect the ductal papilla for visible stones during oral hygiene.
Track flares: time, food triggers, response to massage and warmth.
Attend follow-up imaging or endoscopy as scheduled.
prognosis: Outlook is excellent in most cases. Stones under 3-4 mm pass spontaneously in up to 50% of patients with hydration and sialogogues. Gland-preserving techniques — sialendoscopy alone or combined with transoral or laser approaches — achieve stone-free outcomes in 85-97% of amenable stones and preserve the gland in over 90%. Recurrence rates after successful stone removal are 5-15% over 5 years and depend on hydration, smoking, medications, and presence of systemic risk factors such as hypercalcemia or gout. Submandibular gland excision is curative but removes the gland; long-term consequences include mild dry mouth on the side of surgery, generally well tolerated. Patient-rated quality-of-life scores improve substantially after gland-preserving treatment (Capaccio 2018). Diagnostic delay remains the main driver of poor outcome: prolonged obstruction leads to chronic fibrosis and the need for gland removal.
recoveryTimeline: Conservative management can clear small stones within 2-6 weeks. Sialendoscopy recovery: minor swelling 1-3 days; full normal eating within 1 week. Transoral sialolithotomy: 1-2 weeks of soft diet, full healing 2-4 weeks. Submandibular gland excision: 2-3 weeks off heavy work; small risk of marginal mandibular weakness improving over 3-6 months. ESWL: same-day return to activity, multiple sessions over weeks may be needed.
complications: Acute bacterial sialadenitis with abscess and risk of deep-neck-space spread, particularly to the submandibular space.
Gland fibrosis and atrophy from chronic obstruction, leading to permanent salivary dysfunction.
Ductal stenosis after stone passage or instrumentation, predisposing to further stones.
Marginal mandibular or lingual nerve injury after submandibular gland excision (combined 2-8%).
Facial nerve weakness after parotid surgery (transient 10-30%, permanent 2-5%).
Recurrent stones in 5-15% over 5 years after gland-preserving treatment.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Otolaryngology — Head and Neck Surgery (AAO-HNSF)
supportResources.description: Patient information on salivary gland disease and find-an-ENT directory.

supportResources.name: British Salivary Gland Society
supportResources.description: Specialty group providing patient resources and clinician directory for sialendoscopy in the UK.

supportResources.name: European Sialendoscopy Training Center (ESTC)
supportResources.description: Educational hub with patient-facing summaries and clinician training.

supportResources.name: Cleveland Clinic Salivary Gland Disorders patient page
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of salivary gland disorders including sialolithiasis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Salivary Gland Tumor
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A salivary gland tumor produces a firm, persistent, non-tender mass that does not flare with meals; ultrasound or MRI shows a soft-tissue mass rather than a calcified stone. Biopsy or fine-needle aspiration is needed when malignancy is suspected (facial nerve weakness, fixed mass, lymphadenopathy).

confusedWithConditions.name: Sjogren Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sjögren causes bilateral, painless, recurrent gland swelling with prominent dry eyes and dry mouth and positive autoantibodies (anti-Ro, anti-La). Sialolithiasis is usually unilateral, painful, mealtime-related, and shows a calcification on imaging.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mumps
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Mumps is a viral parotitis with bilateral parotid swelling, fever, and systemic symptoms in unvaccinated children and young adults; salivary stones are unilateral, structural, and recurrent. Mumps IgM serology and history of MMR vaccination help distinguish.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mucocele of Salivary Gland
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A mucocele is a soft fluctuant mucus-filled swelling, usually in the lower lip from minor-gland duct trauma, that fluctuates in size and is bluish through the mucosa; sialolithiasis is firm, mealtime-related, and located in a major gland.

relatedConditions.name: Salivary Gland Tumor
relatedConditions.relevance: Key differential when a gland mass does not resolve between meals; both managed in salivary gland clinics.

relatedConditions.name: Sjogren Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Major autoimmune cause of salivary gland disease and a predisposing factor for stone formation through chronic hyposalivation.

relatedConditions.name: Mumps
relatedConditions.relevance: Viral parotitis is the leading childhood differential for parotid swelling.

relatedConditions.name: Dry Mouth
relatedConditions.relevance: Hyposalivation predisposes to stones and complicates recovery from any salivary disease.

relatedConditions.name: Mucocele of Salivary Gland
relatedConditions.relevance: Minor-gland obstructive lesion managed in the same oral surgery clinics.

faqs.question: What are salivary gland stones?
faqs.answer: Salivary gland stones (sialolithiasis) are calcium-rich deposits that form in a salivary duct or gland and block the flow of saliva. They cause painful swelling that flares with meals and settles between them. About 80-90% form in the submandibular gland under the jaw; most can now be removed without taking out the gland.

faqs.question: What does a salivary stone feel like?
faqs.answer: A salivary stone produces firm, painful swelling under the jaw or in front of the ear that comes on with eating, peaks during the meal, and gradually fades over 1-2 hours. Some patients describe a hard lump that can be felt with the tongue in the floor of mouth or with fingers behind the angle of the jaw.

faqs.question: What causes salivary stones to form?
faqs.answer: Salivary stones form when saliva flow slows and minerals such as calcium phosphate precipitate around a nidus of debris or bacteria. Dehydration, mucus-rich submandibular saliva, anticholinergic medication, smoking, and chronic gland inflammation are the main risk factors. Gout, hypercalcemia, and hyperparathyroidism contribute in some recurrent cases.

faqs.question: How are salivary stones diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines history (mealtime swelling), bimanual examination of the floor of mouth, and imaging. Ultrasound is the first-line modality with 85-94% sensitivity. Plain dental radiographs detect most submandibular stones. Cone-beam or non-contrast CT, sialendoscopy, and MR sialography are used for difficult cases.

faqs.question: Can salivary stones go away on their own?
faqs.answer: Yes — small stones under 3-4 mm can pass spontaneously in up to 50% of patients with increased hydration, sialogogues (sour candies), warm compresses, and gland massage. Larger or symptomatic stones usually need sialendoscopy, ESWL, or surgical removal to clear.

faqs.question: What is sialendoscopy?
faqs.answer: Sialendoscopy is a minimally invasive procedure in which a 0.8-1.6 mm endoscope is passed through the duct opening in the mouth to visualize stones, stenoses, and inflammation. Small stones are retrieved with baskets or graspers; larger ones can be fragmented with a laser. The gland is preserved in over 90% of cases.

faqs.question: Is salivary gland surgery still needed?
faqs.answer: Less often than before. Modern minimally invasive techniques preserve the gland in 80-95% of treated stones. Gland excision is reserved for deep intraglandular stones, end-stage fibrosis, or failure of endoscopic and transoral approaches. When surgery is required, recovery and nerve risks are well managed in experienced centers.

faqs.question: Can salivary stones cause infection?
faqs.answer: Yes. Obstruction allows bacteria to ascend and cause acute sialadenitis with fever, redness, induration, and pus from the ductal opening. Deep-neck-space spread (Ludwig's angina from a submandibular abscess) is a medical emergency. Treatment is antibiotics, hydration, sialogogues, and drainage when an abscess forms.

faqs.question: Do salivary stones come back?
faqs.answer: Recurrence is approximately 5-15% over 5 years after gland-preserving treatment. Risk is higher with persistent dehydration, smoking, anticholinergic medication, or untreated systemic conditions such as hypercalcemia and gout. Hydration, sialogogues, and lifestyle changes reduce recurrence substantially.

faqs.question: Are salivary stones dangerous?
faqs.answer: Most are uncomfortable rather than dangerous, but untreated obstruction can lead to recurrent infections, chronic gland damage, abscess, and deep-neck-space spread. Persistent gland firmness or a non-fluctuating mass requires imaging and sometimes biopsy to exclude tumor.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from sialendoscopy?
faqs.answer: Most patients return to normal eating within a week. Mild gland swelling and a metallic taste are common for 1-3 days. Driving and most desk work resume the next day; vigorous physical activity within 48-72 hours. Follow-up ultrasound at 3 and 12 months checks for residual or recurrent stones.

faqs.question: What is the success rate of ESWL for salivary stones?
faqs.answer: Extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy fragments stones up to 7 mm. Stone-free rates are 35-50% after one session and 60-80% after multiple sessions over weeks. ESWL is better for parotid than submandibular stones and is available in a limited number of centers worldwide.

faqs.question: Are there medications that prevent salivary stones?
faqs.answer: No drug specifically prevents salivary stones. Hydration, sialogogues (citrus drops, sugar-free gum), smoking cessation, and review of anticholinergic medication reduce recurrence. In patients with hypercalcemia or hyperuricemia, treating the underlying cause lowers stone-formation risk.

faqs.question: Do salivary stones affect children?
faqs.answer: Yes, although they account for under 5% of sialolithiasis cases. Pediatric stones are usually submandibular and managed with the same gland-preserving techniques. Recurrent parotitis in children is more often juvenile recurrent parotitis without stones and is treated separately.

faqs.question: Can I eat normally with a salivary stone?
faqs.answer: Most patients can eat soft, well-hydrated foods with smaller bites. Sour or spicy foods often trigger the most pain at the start of a meal. After successful stone removal, normal diet resumes within 1-2 weeks. Long-term dietary restriction is not required.

faqs.question: Are salivary stones related to kidney stones?
faqs.answer: There is a modest association in some studies, particularly with systemic disorders of calcium metabolism (hypercalcemia, primary hyperparathyroidism). Most patients with salivary stones do not develop kidney stones and vice versa, but recurrent stone formers should be screened for shared metabolic causes.

faqs.question: Why does the gland swell at mealtimes?
faqs.answer: Eating stimulates saliva production; when a stone blocks the duct, saliva cannot drain, the gland distends, and pain develops within minutes. Once stimulation stops and saliva pressure falls, the gland gradually empties via leak around the stone and swelling resolves. The pattern is highly specific for stone obstruction.

faqs.question: Does smoking cause salivary stones?
faqs.answer: Smoking reduces salivary flow, alters salivary composition, and is linked to higher rates of recurrent sialolithiasis in observational studies. Stopping smoking is one of the most effective lifestyle changes to prevent recurrence after stone removal.

faqs.question: Can salivary stones be left alone if they are not painful?
faqs.answer: Small asymptomatic stones may be monitored, particularly in elderly patients with significant comorbidity. Symptomatic stones — pain, recurrent swelling, infection — should be treated to prevent progressive gland damage and abscess. Shared decision-making with an ENT specialist is appropriate.

faqs.question: What is the difference between sialolithiasis and sialadenitis?
faqs.answer: Sialolithiasis is the presence of stones in the salivary system; sialadenitis is inflammation or infection of the gland, which can be caused by stones, viruses, or autoimmune disease. Most cases of obstructive sialadenitis in adults are caused by sialolithiasis.

faqs.question: How can I prevent salivary stones from returning?
faqs.answer: Drink 2-3 L water daily, chew thoroughly, use sugar-free citrus drops at meals, stop smoking, review xerogenic medications, maintain oral hygiene, and treat systemic conditions such as gout or hyperparathyroidism. Follow up with ultrasound at 3 and 12 months after treatment to detect any new stone early.

symptomKeywords: swollen gland under jaw at meals
salivary gland swelling on one side
lump in front of ear with eating
